<!-- question-type: prepare -->
### Exercise 5: Quick Recap of Preparation Work

With your group answer the following questions:

**(a)** In one sentence, what is the core trade-off in inventory management for a retailer like David Jones?

**(b)** What are the two biggest issues with the dataset presented in Exercise 2? Justify your choices.

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective** 
Students should:

State the core inventory trade-off in one sentence (stockouts vs overstock).

Evaluate the dataset by selecting two high-impact issues and justifying why they matter for decisions.

Defend choices in a short group discussion (practice managerial reasoning, not just “spotting errors”).

✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

Stockout vs overstock: lost sales/customer harm vs holding/markdown costs.

Data issue vs decision risk: something can “run in R” and still produce a misleading recommendation.

Specificity standard: good critiques point to a column/value and a consequence.


💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)

“For (a), keep it to one sentence. No jargon.”

“For (b), what’s your most decision-relevant issue? Why would management care?”

“Is your issue mainly about code/structure, meaning, or decision risk?”

“If you could fix only one thing before tomorrow’s meeting, what would it be?”

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

Students list issues but don’t justify them.

* Prompt: “So what? What goes wrong if we ignore it?”

:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

::: {.solution-block-body}

**(a)** 

Inventory management is about avoiding stockouts (and consequently lost sales/customer frustration) while avoiding overstock (holding costs/markdowns).

(b) Two biggest dataset issues (example strong pairs + justifications)
There isn’t a single right pair — the key is that students pick issues that materially affect interpretation or computation and justify them clearly.

Here are high-quality answers you can accept, but there are other suitable answers if well justified:

Pair 1 (code/type + meaning):

* Ending Inventory = -20 (backorder) mixes numbers with text → breaks numeric analysis (min/comparisons) and hides how backorders are recorded.
* Timing ambiguity (do deliveries arrive before or after sales each week?) → changes the inventory flow story, so “risky weeks” and recommended orders could be wrong.

Pair 2 (structure + decision risk):

* Promotion mixes Y and Y (Christmas) → unclear whether this is the same treatment; complicates grouping and comparisons.
* Units Sold may be constrained by stockouts → observed sales may understate true demand, so forecasting based on sales can mislead.
:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
